items.title: Why American data centers can't plug in
items.author: Chris Gillett
items.blurb: America's AI buildout is bottlenecked not by power generation but by grid interconnection; the essay argues that reforming the queue with auctions and flexible-load rules could plug data centers in far faster.

items.title: Europe as science superpower: what it will take to rival the US and China
items.author: Elizabeth Gibney
items.blurb: Amid upheaval in US science and geopolitical turmoil, Europe is positioning itself as a research haven—but Nature finds open questions about funding and innovation stand between ambition and rivaling the US and China.

items.title: Will AI spark a scientific renaissance — or a diffuse monoculture?
items.author: Xizhe Zhang
items.blurb: Argues AI's effect on research turns on whether scientists keep prizing originality over speed—it can streamline work across fields but also risks an industrialized 'monoculture' of high-volume, intellectually homogeneous papers.

items.title: EU allies join US pact to break reliance on Chinese AI supply chains
items.blurb: The EU is joining 'Pax Silica,' the US-led alliance coordinating AI-chip supply chains and export controls against China; its architect Jacob Helberg tells the FT the bloc will boost innovation rather than distort markets.

items.title: Why the West stopped making land
items.author: Zigmund Forrest & Maxwell Tabarrok
items.blurb: Western nations stopped reclaiming land from the water half a century ago—not for lack of sites, but because 1970s environmental laws like NEPA and the Clean Water Act made it prohibitively slow and costly.

items.title: America’s data-centre backlash puts the AI boom at risk
items.blurb: Local opposition to data centres is spreading across the US, and The Economist argues the permitting and grid backlash could throttle the AI build-out—an abundance-vs-NIMBY fight echoing the data-centre interconnection problem.

items.title: Losing the War of the Future
items.author: Paul Scharre
items.blurb: Scharre warns that emerging technologies—AI, autonomy, drones—are eroding America's military advantage faster than its institutions are adapting.

items.title: The halo effect: how academic hierarchy undermines peer review and enables fraud
items.author: Wenfeng Wang
items.blurb: A correspondence argues that deference to prestigious authors—the 'halo effect'—weakens peer review and lets fraud slip through, a structural flaw in how science polices itself.

items.title: Make science more reliable: study people as they go about their lives
items.author: John A. List
items.blurb: List argues the behavioural sciences must move past the replication crisis to tackle generalizability—testing whether lab findings hold when people are studied in real life.

items.title: Two Roads to Fast Clinical Trials, and the US Takes Neither
items.author: Alex Tabarrok
items.blurb: Tabarrok contrasts how China runs trials itself and Australia delegates oversight to speed clinical-trial approvals, arguing the US has adopted neither route and remains needlessly slow.

items.title: Silicon Valley’s vision for global AI is flawed: each country needs its own blueprint
items.author: Pelonomi Moiloa
items.blurb: From energy grids to language performance, emerging economies are exposing the limits of a one-size-fits-all American AI strategy as it expands globally, the author argues.

items.title: China imposes trade curbs on dozens of U.S. firms in retaliation for Pentagon blacklist
items.author: Anniek Bao
items.blurb: After the Pentagon added Chinese tech firms to its military-linked list, Beijing put 10 US suppliers on its export-control list and barred 46 defense contractors from procurement—steps analysts read as largely symbolic.

items.title: The Mirage of China’s Military Edge
items.author: Dennis Blair
items.blurb: A former US intelligence chief argues that alarm over China's military and technological rise is overblown and that panic itself is counterproductive to American strategy.

items.title: Europe’s ungoverned space: Military AI and the autonomy that cannot be bought
items.blurb: European defence ministries run on AI infrastructure they do not control or audit; after the US ordered Anthropic to cut off non-US access in June 2026, the column argues Europe faces a sovereignty gap that will take ten to fifteen years to close and outlines three interim steps.

items.title: Writing code versus shipping code: Productivity effects across generations of AI coding tools
items.author: Mert Demirer, Leon Musolff, Liyuan Yang
items.blurb: Studying over 100,000 GitHub developers, the paper finds AI coding agents raise commits by up to 180% but only 30% more shipped releases—the gap reflects human bottlenecks further down the production chain, consistent with the weak-link hypothesis.

items.title: What defines Japan’s national identity?
items.author: Hiroko Yoda
items.blurb: As Japan's birth rate falls and mass immigration accelerates, guest essayist Hiroko Yoda argues that Japan’s identity rests on shared culture rather than ethnicity—and examines how a far-right backlash is beginning to test that idea.

items.title: Will the MATCH Act Change Chip Controls?
items.author: Aqib Zakaria
items.blurb: Analyzes the proposed MATCH Act, which would lock semiconductor export controls into law and press allies to match them, and why China may route around the rules through domestic suppliers.

items.title: How the Glorious Revolution crushed the NIMBYs
items.author: Kara Dimitruk & Ben Southwood
items.blurb: It argues that England's 1688 revolution let Parliament dismantle fragmented property rights and rigid inheritance rules, clearing the way for the investment that drove industrialization.

items.title: Is SMIC N+3's Metal Pitch Smaller than Intel 18A's?
items.blurb: A teardown finding that SMIC's N+3 process hits a smaller metal pitch than Intel 18A but only TSMC N6-class density, achieved through aggressive DUV multi-patterning at a steep cost in complexity.

items.title: How to fix transit construction in America
items.author: Matthew Yglesias et al.
items.blurb: A playbook of concrete fixes to planning, procurement, permitting, and state capacity aimed at closing the gap between America's high transit spending and its poor cost-effectiveness.

items.title: Why the Human Genome's Tangled Physicality May Confound AI
items.author: Philip Ball
items.blurb: The genome behaves as a dynamic, context-dependent regulatory system rather than a linear blueprint, making it hard for AI to predict biology from sequence alone.

items.title: Exclusive: Neuroscience journal editor resigns over automation concerns
items.author: Dalmeet Singh Chawla
items.blurb: An associate editor at Frontiers in Systems Neuroscience resigned after the publisher's automated system overrode his hand-picked peer reviewers, which he reads as a drive to sideline human editors for publication volume.

items.title: How Will Boomer Retirement Affect Jobs for Young Adults?
items.author: Timothy Taylor
items.blurb: As Boomers retire and birth rates fall, US labor-force growth slows toward zero in the 2030s-40s, which Taylor argues should push up wages for young workers entering the market.

items.title: Congress cuts a deal on the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
items.author: Halina Bennet
items.blurb: Lawmakers reach a bicameral agreement on housing legislation even as housing starts decline and homeownership slips in parts of the country.

items.title: How to Survive the AI Shock
items.author: James A. Davidson, Matthew J. Slaughter

items.title: When Workers Lose to AI
items.author: Jacob Leibenluft

items.title: Should People Avoid Whole-Body Screening Info?
items.author: Scott Alexander

items.title: Nature Medicine's June 2026 Benchmark Study Reveals General-Purpose LLMs Outperform FDA-Cleared Clinical AI — and Exposes a Validation Gap Regulators Have Not Closed
items.author: Moe Alsumidaie

items.title: Is the UK improving?
items.author: Tyler Cowen

items.title: Why “China First” Will Fail
items.author: Patricia M. Kim

items.title: Americans Are in Denial About Elder Care
items.author: Stephanie H. Murray

items.title: N.S.A. Lost Access to Powerful A.I. Model Amid Anthropic Dispute
items.author: Dustin Volz and Julian E. Barnes

items.title: Today Was the Day Galileo Caved
items.author: Bob Grant

items.title: The first ticking ‘nuclear clocks’ are here
SpaceX Just Needs the Money
items.author: James Surowiecki

items.title: Music Markets Remain Deglobalized
items.author: Tyler Cowen

items.title: The Mars Delusion
China's Farm Drones: A Trojan Horse Washington Overlooks
Kioxia caps years of uncertainty to become Japan's most valuable company
Merit vs. Tenure: Reforming Federal Firing
Stop Paying More for Less Transit
The strange rocks that wouldn't exist without us
